data.title: Reinsurance Group of America: Growth Focus May Be Backfiring (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Reinsurance Group of America, Incorporated (NYSE:RGA) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing 15% of their value. While it has been aggressive in signing large transactions, mixed performance has increased concerns about the efficacy of its capital allocation efforts. Another mixed quarter in Q2 is only likely to sustain those fears with the stock down over 5% after hours.

I last covered shares of RGA in March, reiterating them as a Buy. Since then, they are up just 2%, missing out on a 12% market rally. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit RGA.

In the company’s second quarter, Reinsurance Group of America earned just $4.72, which missed expectations by $0.83. This was down substantially from the $5.66 it earned in Q1 because RGA experienced unfavorably high claims in U.S. life and healthcare. While claims can be volatile quarter to quarter, especially in large-value life insurance, this continues a string of volatile results, which is all the more concerning given the pace of recent growth. Sometimes when an insurer is growing very fast, it can actually be a warning sign that it is selling policies too cheaply, and that concern is likely to build after these results.

In total, RGA had $256 million of unfavorable claims in Q2, equivalent to about 3% of premiums, a significant variance that more than fully offset ~$160 million of Q1 favorability. This most notably impacted U.S. and Latin American traditional, which barely turned a profit, earning just $4 million, down from $167 million last year. This unit accounts for about a quarter of premiums. A favorable investment environment continues to support U.S. financial solutions (which grew earnings by 20%), and solutions across most geographies. Asia was the only region where it had favorable claims experience in its traditional business.

This large underwriting miss comes as RGA’s traditional premiums are growing 11% from last year on a constant currency basis. They accounted for about 90% of premiums in Q2 because pension-risk transfer premiums inside its financial solutions units are very lumpy. With growth accelerating so much, it is critical to see these recent deals perform well, and another quarter of unfavorable claims will increase worries that growth is so high because the company is underpricing risk.

In fact, even as premiums are rising, the company’s returns have been coming down, adding to the evidence that recent policies are not performing to expectations. RGA generated just a 12.7% return on equity (“ROE”) in Q2, below its 13-15% target. Now, its trailing 12-month ROE is within range but has been ticking down for over a year. At 14.3%, RGA’s ROE is the lowest in two years and likely to decline further.

This compression in ROE is especially concerning because it coincides with RGA relying on more financial leverage, which all else equal should enhance ROE. While RGA has been holding debt to capital constant, it has been increasingly relying on hybrids (debt-like securities that get some equity treatment in rating agency calculations) to finance growth. Frankly, I would like to see RGA slow the pace of new business, digest its recent and pending deals, and bring total debt to capital back below 33%.

Now in Q2, the company deployed just $276 million of capital into new deals, but that is because one of the most significant deals in the company’s history was pending. In addition to announcing earnings, on July 31st, RGA announced it closed its $32 billion reinsurance transaction with Equitable (EQH). Given the deal closed subsequent to quarter end, it did not impact Q2 results. This is a megadeal, with RGA reinsuring 75% of EQH’s individual life block. As part of this deal, RGA will manage 30% of the assets, with 70% still to be managed by EQH’s controlled subsidiary, Alliance Bernstein (AB). RGA is deploying $1.5 billion in capital to support this transaction.

This deal is expected to generate $70 million of operating income in 2025, $160-$170 million in 2026, and $200 million over time once the asset portfolio is fully reinvested according to RGA’s strategy. This implies a 13.5-14% return on capital once ramped, but the deal will be dilutive to ROE until 2027. It is absolutely essential for management credibility to achieve these targets. With many assets still managed by AB, I am cautious on achieving its run-rate and see downside risk to RGA’s modelling, especially given recent performance.

Now back in February, RGA expected to have just $800 million of deployable capital in 2025 after the EQH deal—meaning this one deal accounts for about 2/3 of its new business this year. However given debt movements and how it values its in-force book, RGA now estimates it has $2.3 billion of deployable capital after the EQH deal.

Based on this significant upward revision, it seems like RGA’s appetite for large deals is not diminishing. While this increase is intended to be shown as a positive, speaking to RGA’s capacity for growth, I am not certain it will be perceived that way, as further premium growth may come at the expense of returns, based on recent trends.

Now, as with any insurer, RGA maintains a large investment portfolio, and while it manages the portfolio a bit more aggressively to generate returns, the portfolio is still fairly conservative. RGA has also been benefiting from the rate environment. Its new money investment yield was 6.53% whereas it had a 4.98% portfolio investment yield. Given ongoing reinvestment and new money, this yield was up 22bps from a year ago.

RGA has a $115 billion investment portfolio. $86 billion of this portfolio is in fixed maturity bonds, 94% of which are investment grade. As I said, it is a high-quality portfolio. RGA has a reasonable 3% allocation to alternatives.

Given challenges in commercial real estate (“CRE”), I would note that RGA has $10 billion of commercial mortgage exposure. The portfolio has a 60% loan-to-value and 1.75x debt-service coverage. These are strong ratios. About $1.75 billion of this portfolio is in office where it has a 64% average LTV. Plus, just 11% of the portfolio matures over the next 18 months, reducing rollover risk. I do not see substantial credit risk here.

Alongside results, RGA raised its dividend by 4.5%, giving shares a 2% dividend yield, which is supported by $550 million of liquidity at the holding company. Given its focus on organic growth, capital returns beyond the dividend are limited. Its share count is down only about 1.4% from last year.

Back in March, I assigned a price target of $213, given my view that RGA could generate a 13-14% ROE over the medium term, at the low end of its 13-15% target. With a book value of $156x, this still implies a fair value of at least $203. However, I see an increasing risk that RGA fails to meet this target, given the seemingly dilutive nature of recent deals. We also know that the EQH deal will struggle to achieve return targets for at least a year.

With such fast growth, rising all-in leverage, and questionable deal economics, the RGA investment thesis feels increasingly broken, and it is difficult for me to see a near-term change in this dynamic. Management has an increasing credibility problem, and it will take several quarters to resolve this. As such, I see shares likely to be stuck in the $175-$190 range, essentially haircutting fair value by 10-15% to reflect this added overhang.

As such, I am moving shares to a Hold. After this quarter, I struggled to continue to defend RGA’s performance; indeed, I defended it too long. With so many other options in the market, I see no compelling reason to buy shares at this juncture, and I would stay on the sidelines in RGA.

data.title: Amazon Q2: Almost Flawless Performance, Misunderstood By The Market
data.description:
A huge e-commerce event, more than 1 million robots, various AI-related features, high viewership for the NASCAR Cup on its streaming service, and advances in its satellite Internet project. That's not all, but these are factors present in Amazon.com, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:AMZN) thesis and its Q2 results.

It can be overwhelming to try to envision Amazon's future, as there are so many divisions and points that can generate up-selling, cross-selling, and efficiency amid secular trends that it is difficult to have a consolidated view of Amazon, and we often miss some points. Much of this ends up becoming optionality. If in 10 years Amazon is one of the main beneficiaries of trends that are currently very intangible, such as humanoid robots, great, because we don't need to incorporate that into the calculation for its valuation to make sense.

Given all these positive characteristics, which showed good progress in Q2, along with the beat in revenue and EPS, I imagined that Amazon shares would be rising in after-hours trading, as was the case with Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT), which also surprised positively and posted substantial gains.

However, the market punished AMZN stock for its below-expectations guidance for Q3, falling about -4% in after-hours trading (at least now as I write this analysis). And even though it's not so encouraging in some ways, it ends up creating a window to accumulate more stocks and reiterate the Buy rating for a company that continues to perform very well, has a lot to gain from secular trends, perhaps even more than other companies, and still has a very interesting margin of safety.

It Seems That The Market Is Overreacting to Amazon's Earnings

In Q2, Amazon reported revenue of $167.7 billion, while estimates were $162 billion. A good beat of 3%. Adjusted EPS was $1.68 against $1.33 expected, a very interesting beat of more than 20%. Furthermore, as the table below shows, the midpoint of guidance for Q3 revenue was $3.5 billion above market expectations.

The problem for the market was operating income. As the midpoint of guidance was $18 billion, and the market expected $19.4 billion, the reaction was negative. But note that the guidance range goes up to $20.5 billion, meaning that even without beating the guidance itself, it would be possible to exceed these expectations. For comparison, Q2 operating income was $19.2 billion, a strong growth of over 30%, while the guidance itself considered a top of $17.5 billion.

Of course, I'm not saying that the company will always exceed the market or its own outlook, nor that this miss in operating income guidance is low, but note that this makes it seem like an overreaction on the part of the market, since there are so many positive points in the quarter and yet the stock is being punished. Not to mention the good reaction of companies with a similar profile, such as Microsoft.

To be honest, even if it were a slight miss in revenue and EPS, it would not make much difference to the long-term thesis, as the operational and positive signs from various initiatives show that Amazon is on track to continue unlocking a lot of value for shareholders.

Thinking Long Term, Q2 Earnings Were Great

The table below suggests to me that earnings were very good. Revenue in North America grew 11%, above recent quarters, as did the International segments. AWS maintained momentum with 17%. Everything is in good shape.

However, that is not the main reason I liked the quarter. For me, one of the main areas where the company will unlock value is in margins. Note how North America's operating margin has already reached 7.5%, a margin close to Q4 2024 but without the boost from seasonality. The same positive movement occurred for the International segment, which has already surpassed last Q4's margin. This illustrates that revenue is moving toward a more profitable mix, such as advertising, services, and subscriptions, which grew more than segments such as online stores, and shows that efficiency gains are ongoing.

The most modest aspect of the result was AWS's operating margin, which fell to around 33%, a significant decline from what the company had been reporting in recent quarters. But this is not so alarming because it was already on the radar. In the Q1 earnings call, the CFO answered a question about AWS's margin in a way that suggested some pressure throughout 2025:

“We did a strong quarter in AWS, as you mentioned, the margin performance. I would attribute it to the strong growth that we're seeing coupled with the impact of some continued investment we're making in innovation and technology, give you some examples. So, we invest in software and process improvements and it ends up optimizing our server capacity, which helps our infrastructure cost. We've been developing more efficient network using our low cost custom networking gear. We're working to maximize the power usage in our existing data centers, which both lowers our costs and also reclaims power for other newer workloads.

And we're also seeing the impact of advancing custom silicon like Graviton. It provides lower costs not only for us, but also for our customers, the better price performance for them. But you're right, margins are impacted by a lot of things, including our level of investment, competitive pricing, the mix of Generative AI services as they're ramping up will continue to evolve over the years to come. So, we do have a lot of investment in infrastructure going on and planned for the second-half of the year. So, that will -- we'll start to see the impact of that. But we're happy with the performance of the team with generating cost savings. And it's a big focus as well as expanding the services and features for customers.”

And if we compare, AWS's growth was not as impressive as Azure's, but that does not invalidate the fact that AWS is also (perhaps even better than its peers) very well positioned to continue riding the secular trend of AI and cloud growth.

And that's not the only reason to have Amazon; in fact, as mentioned at the beginning of the analysis, there are countless reasons. In its earnings release, Amazon cited some of the key events of the quarter:

  • Biggest Prime Day event ever;
  • Expansion of same-day and next-day delivery;
  • New partnerships;
  • Launched GenAI tools, expanded Alexa+ Early Access;
  • Increased Project Kuiper's satellite internet fleet with two successful launches;
  • Had 2 million viewers per race in Prime Video’s NASCAR Cup;
  • Innovated with Amazon Nova (AI platform to build AI agents and similars); and
  • Introduced Vulcan, Amazon’s robot with sight and touch.

And that’s not all, there is an even longer list to check out and delve into to understand each Amazon tool, initiative, and/or robot.

Vulcan Is An Example Of The Future And The Strength Of Amazon

All of this shows me that Amazon's revenue and margin expansion is far from over. Vulcan is a very good example of this thesis. Unlike humanoid robots such as Tesla's (TSLA) Optimus, it does not attract as much attention for its appearance, nor does it seem as disruptive as it really is. But it turns out that in addition to being very disruptive, it also has the characteristic of bringing more efficiency to the company.

Its main difference is that with the use of AI, Vulcan has the “power of touch” to better handle products and even learn from its own mistakes. This touch that Vulcan has was something that even Amazon engineers were skeptical about achieving, something that seemed very difficult, but now it is there and working.

The trend of AI continuing to evolve greatly helps Amazon's revenue and margin prospects, and as I mentioned, it is even difficult to trace all the implications. Efficiency in logistics and improving AWS services and those provided to third-party sellers are more obvious, but we can still consider optionalities such as the evolution of content production for Prime Video, or adding even more features that allow for the charging of subscription services.

In short, Amazon is one of the leading companies in capturing all these innovative trends. The future is truly promising for the company, and this quarter shows that the company knows this and knows how to execute very well.

In fact, in addition to classic risks such as uncertainties regarding tariffs and competition from players such as Mercado Libre (MELI) for the e-commerce division and with Microsoft in the cloud area, one of the main concerns for the thesis, in my view, is that Amazon will become “too big,” as this could trigger even more regulatory problems and other dilemmas.

Amazon Also Offers A Good Margin Of Safety

Even amid this movement that prices companies positioned in these technology trends very well, Amazon manages to offer an interesting margin of safety. While companies like Microsoft are trading above their historical price-to-earnings average, Amazon is trading below it.

Note in the table below that even though it still has a lot to unlock in its fundamentals, multiples such as price-to-earnings, EV-to-EBITDA, and even price-to-cash-flow are substantially lower than their average over the last five years.

Being simplistic in valuation, think that a multiple of 35x forward earnings or even a little less is fair for Amazon. If this multiple remains constant and net income continues to grow above 10% in the coming years, this is more or less the expected total return, since if well priced, the stock price in the long term follows earnings.

One of the relevant points worth mentioning is that it does not seem feasible to imagine that the company will return to trading at astronomical multiples, since now its fundamentals are much more mature, with the operating margin of all segments having already advanced well, i.e., we will not see the margin of the international division double again, but rather an advance more in line with its current maturity.

The Bottom Line

Amazon is one of the leading companies for exposure to long-term trends in a very efficient and diversified manner. As Q2 earnings showed, the company is not only already delivering significant progress in fundamentals, but also has many projects that will continue to yield results in the coming years and decades.

Therefore, I reiterate my buy rating for Amazon.

data.title: This Is Why It's Time To Trim Your AMD Stock Ahead Of Q2 Earnings (Downgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD) stock, it is up by 30% in price.

AMD has earnings coming up on August 5th, and the reality is that even with a modest beat and raise, the stock is going to experience a minor pullback. That's why I trimmed my AMD position by 1/3 on Monday July 28, and sold the rest on Wednesday July 30. My total return since buying in H1 2025 was about 75% for the final closed stake.

It's best not to be greedy—those are world-class returns and sentiment-value investors like myself know that the massive AMD value opportunity is over and was prime in March following an extended downtrend. AMD will almost certainly continue to rise over the next few years, but with volatility. I think H2 2025 will be a period of potentially a tech and general equity market pullback/consolidation period before a stronger 2026. I'm rotating proceeds from trimmed AI names into more defensive and cyclical value plays right now as a result.

Here's What You Need To Know

AMD stock is currently trading at a 14-week RSI value of 73, and the stock price is now significantly higher than the 50-week moving average in price. This is really a very obvious moment to trim your AMD stock.

The forward non-GAAP P/E and forward P/S ratios are now above the five-year average, with forward revenue growth a substantial decline from the five-year average (-26.5%), with forward diluted EPS growth a -21% difference from the five-year average. In short, AMD is fundamentally overvalued. Reduce your stake now as gains from here will be momentum based (value investors have already locked in profits and are looking elsewhere).

AMD is suitable for long-term holding but the likelihood of a drawdown in the near term is high before continued capital appreciation. 2026 will likely be strong for AMD, but I'm really anticipating an equity pullback in H2 if rates stay elevated and the Treasury General Account ("TGA") is about to be refilled with about $500 billion. A liquidity drain like this through H2 2025 has a dual purpose of refinancing the government's checking account and also stabilizing valuations before rate cuts in 2026 and a real economy, stable (not sentiment-driven) bull market (likely around the midterms).

In other words, you should be trimming your AI stocks now and raising cash (I'm currently at about 25-30%) and investing in undervalued names that are less prone to heavy volatility or defensive stocks like Eli Lilly (LLY), which has lower drawdown risk but strong 2026 potential, especially amid orforglipron (oral GLP-1) arriving to market next year. It's time to subtly reduce, rotate, and hold cash to strike amid more favorable tech valuations in H2—AMD included.

The reason that the above chart says "Trim" and not "Close" is that AMD's medium-term growth thesis is very much intact. This is not like mid-2024 when the valuation was extreme, it's moderately overextended. The pullback should be moderate, and then medium-term strong capital appreciation of 15% per annum is logical to expect. That said, that's not enough for top investors and I closed my position accordingly.

Operational Factors & Catalysts To Monitor At Q2 2025 Next Week

In a bull outcome, we might see the stock etch higher after Q2 earnings on August 5, but the valuation makes this somewhat improbable or just irrational from the market if it occurs. A strong beat and raise would be required, and some positive news on China like license approvals and renewed shipments would help. AI demand is soaring though, as major hyperscalers continue to double down on AI capex, so I don't expect any underperformance really. The bear case is a negligible risk (earnings miss and lowered guidance).

In my opinion, even if AMD delivers revenue and EPS in-line with expectations or even slightly above, the stock will take a moderate tumble. I think management will deliver a guidance raise, and it could be substantial, but consensus already reflects quite strong expectations, and the probability of raised guidance is already reflected in the stock price. That's clear when a 14-week RSI goes up above 70.

The weak PC market could also be a meaningful drag on overall performance that constrains further positive sentiment. Additional inventory write-downs from China would be a bearish quality, but this is not likely, as CEO Lisa Su has outlined that the China issue was "well-contained" and already guided for an $800 million inventory charge for MI308 GPUs in Q2. Realistically, we should expect no extra inventory charges above the $800 million previously outlined.

Conclusion: Hold

In my opinion, AMD stock is too expensive right now. It's definitely not a buy as the valuation has become overextended. I sold my entire position, but may buy it back if the valuation becomes more appealing in the next six months. I still think a 12-month price target of $200 is achievable, which was my old take-profit target before closing out ~$180. For long-term investors, trimming is fine, but for active traders and value players like myself, it's best to deploy the capital elsewhere right now because the implied 12-month upside is only 11% in a bull case.

There are other stocks I'm looking at right now which have better value prospects, like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDY), AppLovin (APP) (sentiment-based value, not intrinsic, even though forward fundamental growth is remarkable), and I am considering increasing my Nebius (NBIS) position as the stock has shown better sentiment-based value right now compared to the more dominant semi and AI stocks.

The macroeconomic climate for H2 2025 is still somewhat uncertain as I alluded to earlier in this analysis. My cash position is currently 29.5%, which is a bit high, but positions me well for any contraction that may occur in tech valuations soon.

data.title: JEPQ: GPIX Has A Surprisingly Important Yet Small Edge Over This Fund
data.description:
The value of any investment is impossible to compare without using a comparative framework at times. While most investors have both short-term and long-term goals, finding the best-performing investments to reach an individual's objectives is still important, even when people's goals will obviously be different based on a number of factors.

While the JPMorgan funds, such as the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:JEPQ), have been the gold standard in the industry for some time, there are increasing signs that there are better options for investors. Covered-call funds have evolved significantly over the last decade as investor demand for consistent and substantive income has continued to increase, and the more simplistic approach of selling at-the-money index calls against nearly the entirety of an ETF's holdings used by the Global X funds, such as the Global X NASDAQ-100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD), has proven to offer only mediocre income and total returns. The slightly more sophisticated strategy of using data-based models to sell out-of-the-money calls such as JEPQ, which was listed in May of 2022, has empirically shown to be a better approach to maximizing income and total returns.

The latest covered-call funds that have been developed, which have consistently outperformed both the Global X Funds and JP Morgan options-based ETFs, include the Goldman Sachs options-based investments, such as the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:GPIX).

Since GPIX was listed in October of 2023, the fund has offered investors total returns of 48.63 percent, while JEPQ has offered investors total returns of 47.47 percent, but this Goldman fund has consistently outperformed JEPQ since January as well.

I last wrote about JEPQ in March of 2025, and I rated the fund a strong buy. Today, I am downgrading this JP Morgan investment to a buy. While JEPQ remains a solid option, the fund's failure to capture more of the upside in the Nasdaq-100 index that this ETF closely tracks compared to peers such as GPIX is notable. The option strategies used by some newer developed covered-call funds are showing to be more effective than JEPQ's approach.

JEPQ's options strategy is to sell monthly equity-linked notes against the Nasdaq-100 index, which this fund closely tracks. The ETF sells options with 2.5-7.5 percent out-of-the-money. The strike price of the monthly calls sold varies based on volatility levels, and the fund uses a data-driven approach that has worked to determine what strike prices to target each month. JEPQ's ELN holdings generally vary between 15-20 percent of the holdings of the overall fund.

GPIX uses a unique options strategy that involves significant discretion and flexibility. The fund sells calls against between 25-75 percent of the equity positions the ETF holds, and the managers also adjust the strike prices for the monthly European index options that are being written against the GPIX's holdings. This approach is particularly important when volatility levels are less predictable, as has been the case recently in equity markets.

This is the main reason GPIQ has significantly outperformed JEPQ during market rallies, such as at the end of 2024 and in May and June of 2025. While all three funds have performed similarly during market sell-offs, the Goldman investments have performed significantly better during multiple rallies in just the last year. While JEPQ has paid out nearly $6.19 of income during this same timeframe, GPIX has paid out income of $4.12 over the last year. GPIX has a more conservative annual income target of 8 percent, so the payout approach is different as well.

GPIX's managers have also simply been more adept at using a nuanced approach to writing calls to consistently capture more of the upside in the Nasdaq-100 index than JEPQ's managers.

GPIX appears to have a better model and slightly more capable managers than JEPQ, although the JP Morgan ETF continues to achieve the fund's stated goal of 6-9 percent a year of income and 1-2 percent of total returns.

All investment theses have risks, and the data for GPIX remains limited to less than 2 years. JEPQ is also taking a more conservative options-based approach that could lead to the fund outperforming during an extended sell-off over multiple years, although such a scenario remains unlikely given how well large-cap tech earnings and projections continue to hold up. JEPQ is also consistently exceeding the fund's stated goals of offering investors 1-2 percent a year in total returns and 6-9 percent annually in payouts.

JEPQ is a solid fund, and the investment should continue to be able to continue to meet or exceed the ETF's impressive goals. Still, GPIX has shown to offer investors notably more upside during market rallies. While the JP Morgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF remains a viable option for investors, GPIX's approach should continue to produce better results for investors regardless of market conditions.

data.title: Figma: Investors Are Mixing Up Imagination From Reality
data.description:
Shares of Figma (NYSE:FIG) have seen incredible momentum on the first day of trading, with shares more than tripling as investors want in on this hottest thing in town. With momentum and sales multiples being sky-high, the company has a long runway for growth. This is confirmed by the fact that smarter money was willing to buy this business for a huge price in the past, driven by the superiority of the products.

While I am very cautious given the momentum displayed, and thus not advocating a long position here, a short position might be dangerous as well, making it rather easy to watch developments take hold from the sidelines here.

Where Teams Come Together To Create Great Digital Products & Experiences

The header of this paragraph is in essence what Figma aims to achieve. Billions of people use apps, websites and other digital experiences created through Figma, as the company claims that design is everyone's business.

The business was started back in 2012 with a vision to eliminate the gap between imagination and reality, allowing people to get ideas out of one's head onto the screen. The company actually has 13 million monthly active users, actually two-thirds of which are no designers.

That is key as often design was originated in silos which made that designers are not directly involved with project managers, marketers, writers and developers. The goal of its solutions is to integrate design and make it accessible to all the members in the group.

The traction of the business and superiority of solutions was picked up by Adobe (ADBE) which in 2022 offered $20 billion for the firm, only to see the transaction fall apart in 2023 due to regulatory concerns, but that did not slow the business down.

Valuation and IPO Thoughts

Figma aimed to sell 37 million shares in a preliminary price range between $30 and $32 per share, with final pricing set at $33 per share, just above the higher end of the preliminary guidance. Some 12.5 million shares were sold by the business, with the remainder sold by selling shareholders, as the company raised some $412 million in gross proceeds from the offering, that is excluding exercising of the overallotment option.

More than 487 million shares are outstanding following the offering, consisting of two (voting) classes, which grants the business a $16 billion valuation at the offer price, coincidentally a bit less than the purchase price offered by Adobe. This includes a $1.5 billion net cash position ahead of the offering, with these holdings increasing a long way to the $2 billion mark, after factoring in the offering proceeds, implying a $14 billion operating asset valuation.

The numbers are a bit hard to read, with 2023 sales reported at $505 million, on which a GAAP operating loss of $73 million was reported. Revenues rose more than 48% to $749 million in 2024 as a huge $877 million operating loss was reported, this relating to massive stock-based compensation expenses, which seems a bit like a one-off.

Momentum remains very strong, with first quarter sales up 46% to $228 million, already trending at a run rate of $900 million. The company has posted solid operating profits already, reported at $40 million for the quarter, which is a real achievement. Preliminary second quarter sales are seen up at a midpoint of $248 million, suggesting 40% growth on an annual basis.

Contrary to the strong earnings in the first quarter, GAAP operating profits are seen around break-even levels, as revenues already trend around a billion, suggesting that the market is valuing this play at 14 times sales, amidst 40% revenue growth and break-even results reported.

Needless to say, that is before shares tripled on the first day of trading, granting the business a roughly $50 billion equity valuation at levels just over the $100 mark, and a rather similar enterprise valuation. This values the business at roughly 50 times annualised sales, dazzling multiples.

Concluding Thoughts

Given the valuation discussion above, it is clear that one of the biggest risks might be that of valuation. Needless to say, growth is impressive, cash holdings are huge and amidst break-even results, or even modest profits reported, financials concerns are not high on the list of investors.

Other than the key valuation risk, there are other risks including that of reliance on key staff, the existence of dual-class shares, that of slower growth, and, of course, competitive offerings. One of these competitors is, of course, Adobe, which saw the value in the firm and the strength of the business, and with a $150 billion equity valuation, Adobe remains a large and bigger competitor.

The pricing action soon after the public offering really suggests some sort of crazy momentum, this being hyped up as the hottest thing in town. While the business is still relatively small, it is very well understood that smart money understands how potentially disruptive the business is, otherwise Adobe would not have offered $20 billion for the business three years ago, when the revenue base was much smaller (and an even higher sales multiple was applied to the business).

So hence, shares are not automatic short based on sky-high multiples, given that an expert business like Adobe was willing to really pay up for the business three years ago, as market share gains of Figma away from Adobe indicate the potential. Even now, at a 50 times sales multiple and respectable market capitalization, the valuation is just a third of the valuation of Adobe here (which, however, is a much bigger and profitable firm).

Given all this, I am in doubt. I am not daring to short the shares, yet momentum is clearly showing signs of exuberance, making a long position highly speculative here. Thus, some propositions are too hard to value, and this one makes it easy to watch from the sidelines, as I am looking to get more clues about the growth and longevity of growth of the business.

data.title: Apple Q3: $100 Billion In Buybacks, $9 Billion In CapEx - Why I'm Not Buying
data.description:
Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) has just released its quarterly results, with revenues up 10% and earnings per share up 12%. But if this is really the future of Cupertino, then Houston, we have a problem. Because while Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Alphabet (GOOGL) are investing billions to secure front-row seats in the AI race, Apple prefers to continue buying back its own shares.

The illusion of a strong quarter

Apple reported revenues of $94.04 billion, up +9.6% from last year and well above analysts' consensus (89.3B). EPS rose to $1.57, compared to the expected $1.43. Wall Street reacted with a +2.6% rise in after-hours trading (at the time of writing), but I believe this rebound is more emotional than rational.

Why? Because the numbers are strong, but the context empties them of meaning.

The iPhone generated $44.6 billion (+13% y/y), beating estimates thanks to aggressive discounts in China and promotions spread everywhere. But there was no real product innovation: it was a race to the bottom on prices to defend market share. The Mac surprised on the upside (+15%, to $8.05 billion), but it is cyclical, volatile, and non-core. The iPad, on the other hand, fell 8.1% ($6.58 billion), yet another sign of fatigue on what is now a marginal line.

The wearables business is also contracting (-8.6%, to 7.40B): and this is where I start to worry. Because when “ecosystem” products lose their appeal, it means that the loyalty cycle is weakening.

Only Services are holding up: 27.4 billion (+13%), with high margins and excellent cash flow. But they are under attack on several fronts, including the DOJ lawsuit over the Google deal, antitrust investigations in Europe, and the Digital Markets Act, which requires systems to be open. And if the App Store becomes contestable, Cupertino's most stable source of profits will collapse.

Apple beat estimates, yes. But thanks to a favorable environment (dollar devaluation, promotional boosts, low expectations). Not thanks to a new product, technological discontinuity, or visionary CapEx. And for me, that's a huge problem.

CapEx at a minimum, buybacks at a maximum - What game is it playing?

There is one figure that says it all: CapEx. In the quarter, Apple spent $9.4 billion on CapEx and $22.9 billion on buybacks. Almost three times as much to buy back shares instead of building the future, building barriers, and positioning itself among the top big tech companies in the AI race. Why?

At a time when Meta is investing over $40 billion a year in AI and data centers, Alphabet is openly talking about a “super cycle of spending,” and Microsoft is funding OpenAI to internalize the next generation of cognitive platforms, Apple is doing the opposite.

It's not innovating. Is it optimizing?

The comparison is discouraging; Cupertino seems more like a hedge fund than a tech company. Not a word about new infrastructure, chips, servers, or proprietary GPUs. The “Apple Intelligence” shown at WWDC is a nice UI on top of open source models. An elegant wrapper, but I'm sorry...nothing more.

And while others are investing capital to secure future margins, Apple prefers to inflate EPS today. It's legitimate. It's also smart, in a way. But is it a business plan? No.

Apple Intelligence? More marketing than revolution

The word “AI” is everywhere in the Big Tech lexicon, but in Apple's case, it has a different flavor. It almost seems like a patch for a struggling narrative. We've been saying this for months now.

At WWDC25, Apple presented “Apple Intelligence” as a suite integrated into its OS. The interface is beautiful, as smooth as ever, but there is no fundamental innovation, no proprietary large language model. Advanced users understood this immediately: beneath the surface lies a partnership with OpenAI (and perhaps also with Anthropic in the future). Apple does not have its own AI fundamentals. It uses those of others, like a luxury retailer that assembles outsourced products.

And here emerges the real divide with those who are truly driving this revolution.

  • Meta has its own complete stack (Llama 3) and releases it open-source to influence the entire ecosystem.
  • Google dominates with Gemini, vertically integrated into all its services.
  • Microsoft funds OpenAI, but integrates Copilot into Office, Azure, GitHub, Windows... every product is already AI-native.
  • Apple? Simply... behind.

What Apple calls “AI,” the others had 12 months ago. And while everyone else is accelerating, Apple is limiting itself to aesthetics and saving EPS.

In this context, it is legitimate to ask: if the next computing platform is truly AI-based, will Apple stand by and watch? At best, it will focus on luxury aesthetics...

5 major challenges

Apple is not in crisis. But it is facing a phase in which its growth, its role, and even its business model are being questioned. According to the company, it faces at least 5 major challenges:

  1. AI without its own engine: as we mentioned earlier, Apple has introduced “Apple Intelligence,” but it relies on OpenAI. It does not have a proprietary model. In an era where owning the model means owning the user experience, this is a structural handicap. The other big players (Google, Meta, Microsoft) are playing as hosts. Apple is playing as a guest.
  2. CapEx vs. buyback: $9.4 billion in capital expenditures in the last quarter versus $110 billion in buybacks last year. This is a snapshot of a company that prefers “financial engineering” to innovation. In a context of technological transformation, it is a strategy of a former champion (see above).
  3. Increasingly uncomfortable dependence on China and India: Apple has recovered in China only thanks to aggressive discounts. But the geopolitical climate is tense, and India, where part of production is being shifted, has just imposed 25% tariffs. The flexible supply chain suddenly becomes vulnerable.
  4. Static products: Mac sales are growing again, but iPad sales are declining. Updates are minor, launches have little impact. There are no game-changing devices. Vision Pro? Still a niche product. And the risk is that the future will arrive sooner from a competitor (such as Meta with Ray-Ban AI, or Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) with on-device AI).
  5. Regulators, App Store, and margins under pressure: Services are the engine of growth, but they are also under special scrutiny. The App Store is under regulatory siege in Europe and the U.S. Extraordinary margins could be squeezed if Apple were to open up (by law) to alternative payment systems.

Together, these five challenges paint a picture of Apple no longer leading the curve of change, but rather undergoing it. It is no longer the pinnacle of tech innovation: it has become a safe haven for fans, but less so for those seeking sustainable growth over the next five years.

The Apple paradox: beautiful, beloved, but irrelevant

Apple is still a money-making machine, no doubt about it, turning in record profits. Analysts love it, investors love it, but if we take away the affection and nostalgia, one fact remains: it no longer sets the tech agenda.

When Microsoft speaks, the market listens to understand where AI is headed. The same happens when Meta invests: new avenues open up in devices and distribution. When Alphabet restructures, it reinvents entire sectors.

(You can read what I wrote about Meta, Microsoft, and Alphabet at the links above).

And Apple? It releases incremental updates. It improves chips. It optimizes margins. It does buybacks. But it no longer changes the rules of the game.

The risk today is not a collapse, but rather boredom. The risk is a company that continues to live in the past while others write the next chapter. And we investors cannot afford to remain in the previous chapter.

Conclusion: a wonderful company, but an overvalued stock

Apple has never been in question as a company. The numbers speak for themselves: $94 billion in revenue in Q3, profits up 12%, gross margin at 46.5%, and a user base that continues to expand. A perfect, well-oiled machine capable of generating tons of cash every quarter. But that doesn't mean the stock is a good investment today.

Today, Apple is trading at 30 times expected earnings, with a PEG of around 3 and an EV/EBITDA close to 22. Expected EPS growth? +8% annually. R&D and CapEx spending? Just $9.4 billion, while in the same quarter it authorized another $100 billion in buybacks. In other words, Apple is buying its own past, while other companies are investing in the future (AI, as we all know by now).

Meta, Microsoft, Alphabet: all are investing billions quarter after quarter to build new platforms, data centers, language models, and mixed reality. Apple, on the other hand, promises “Apple Intelligence” without giving any details, trails behind the AI train, and focuses its energies on efficiency, optimization, and customer loyalty. But when will this innovation arrive? How much longer must we wait? Excellent qualities... but they are no longer enough to justify multiples as a visionary technology leader.

Here is the crux of my argument: Apple today is neither a value stock nor a growth stock. It is a premium stock that is built on solid foundations, certainly, but one that trades at exponential growth multiples without actually delivering it. The risk is not that Apple will collapse tomorrow, but that it will drag on for years with mediocre performance, while investors' capital is eroded by a lack of leadership and saturation of its core business.

Personally, I haven't owned Apple for at least a couple of quarters. I wasn't convinced at the beginning of the year, and I'm still not convinced today. For those buying today, I don't think there is enough margin of safety and value.

My rating is Sell. Not because Apple is weak, but because the stock is simply too strong compared to what it can realistically offer.

data.title: Amazon: Buy The Earnings Dip
data.description:
Analyzing Amazon's Q2 2025 Report

In light of its Q2 2025 earnings, Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock is trading down by around 5%, despite a triple-play quarter from the tech conglomerate:

  • Net Sales: $167.7B [up +13% y/y, vs. est. $162.1B]
  • Diluted EPS: $1.68 per share [up +33% y/y, vs. est. $1.32 per share]
  • Q3 Net Sales Guidance: $174-179.5B [+10-13% y/y, vs. est. $173.2B].

Now, on a segment level, Amazon's retail ecosystem growth ticked up, alongside persistent momentum in Amazon's twin growth engine of AWS [Cloud] ($30.9B, up +17.5% y/y) and Ads ($15.7B, up +23% y/y).

While Amazon's sales numbers are impressive, AWS' Q2 growth rate did not accelerate as we've seen from hyperscaler rivals like Google (GOOGL) Cloud Platform and Microsoft (MSFT) Azure in recent days. In my mind, AMZN's post-ER stock weakness is a direct consequence of this particular figure.

Another possible explanation for Mr. Market's negative reaction to the print could be the precipitous decline in Amazon's TTM free cash flow to $18.2B. Yes, other big tech giants have recorded similarly large drops in FCF amid the ongoing AI infrastructure spending boom; however, Mr. Market is likely overlooking those due to corresponding green shoots in top-line growth within their cloud businesses. Interestingly, Amazon's CEO, Andy Jassy, is quite optimistic about his company's AI prospects:

Our conviction that AI will change every customer experience is starting to play out as we’ve expanded Alexa+ to millions of customers, continue to see our shopping agent used by many millions of customers, launched AI models like DeepFleet that optimize productivity paths for our 1M+ robots, made it much easier for software developers to write code with Kiro (our new agentic IDE), launched Strands to make it easier to build AI agents, and released Bedrock AgentCore to enable agents to be operated securely and scalably. Our AI progress across the board continues to improve our customer experiences, speed of innovation, operational efficiency, and business growth, and I’m excited for what lies ahead.

Now, near-term stock market gyrations are impossible to predict; however, Amazon's Q2 2025 performance is solid, and from a long-term standpoint, AMZN stock remains an enticing prospect.

Amazon Fair Value And Expected Return

Considering Amazon's stronger-than-expected Q2 2025 report and forward guidance, I am lifting our model's revenue base to $715B [FY2025E revenue] for today's valuation exercise. Furthermore, I am sticking to reasonable assumptions for future growth [5-year sales CAGR: 10%] and steady-state margins [optimized FCF margin: 20%].

All other assumptions from our previous assessment have been retained, but please feel free to share any questions in the comments section below.

Here's our updated valuation model for Amazon:

As per TQI's Valuation Model, Amazon's fair value estimate is ~$214 per share, which is only slightly lower than the current market price of AMZN.

Now, predicting where a stock will trade in the short term is impossible; however, over the long run, a stock will track its business fundamentals and obey the immutable laws of money. If the interest rates were to return to artificially low levels (i.e., ZIRP), higher equity multiples would be justifiable. However, I work with the assumption that interest rates will eventually track the long-term average of ~5%. Inverting this number, we get a trading multiple of ~20x (P/FCF).

Assuming a base case exit multiple of 20x P/FCF, we reach a 5-year price target of ~$456 per share for AMZN, which implies a CAGR return of ~15.2%.

Final Thoughts

With Amazon's business in fine fettle and its 5-year expected CAGR return exceeding TQI's investment hurdle rate, I view the post-ER dip in Amazon stock as a buying opportunity for long-term investors.

Key Takeaway: I rate Amazon stock a Buy in the $220s, with preference for staggered accumulation.

Thank you for reading, and happy investing! Please share any questions, thoughts, and/or concerns in the comments section below or DM me.

data.title: Madrigal Pharmaceuticals: Rezdiffra Expansion Avenues Shift Focus To Long Term
data.description:
Madrigal Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:MDGL) has been doing quite well in terms of sales with its FDA-approved drug Rezdiffra [resmetirom], which has been approved for the treatment of adults with non-cirrhotic non-alcoholic steatohepatitis [NASH] with moderate to advanced liver fibrosis [scarring] done alongside diet and exercise. The truth is that this company is building up sales with this drug, having obtained Q1 of 2025 revenues of $137.3 million. I believe that the value of this company is only beginning to take off because of how big the metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis [MASH] market is expected to be. If FDA approval was already given, then what value could be left for shareholders? Well, it is because the company has several expansion opportunities in play.

The first expansion opportunity to note of, which, if it does end up happening, could end up boosting shareholder value. The Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use [CHMP] of the European Medicines Agency [EMA] gave a positive opinion on recommending approval of Rezdiffra for the treatment of adults with non-cirrhotic NASH with moderate to advanced liver fibrosis. Even better, the European Commission [EC] decision on whether or not it should end up being marketed in the European territory is expected in August of 2025. This marks a huge catalyst and inflection point for investors to watch.

A second expansion opportunity would be the ongoing phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES study, which is targeting compensated NASH cirrhosis patients. This trial is ideal because it would not only allow the full approval of Rezdiffra for non-cirrhotic MASH patients, but it would also allow the company to expand its label targeting compensated MASH cirrhosis patients. The downside is that the release of results from this phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES study is not expected until 2027.

The third and last expansion opportunity deals with the company entering into a global license agreement with CSPC Pharmaceutical Group Limited to in-license oral small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist and orforglipron derivative SYH2086. More about this directly below, but the end goal is to combine this with Rezdiffra to improve anti-fibrotic activity in MASH patients.

The Licensing Of Oral Small Molecule SYH2086 Into The Fray For MASH

As I noted above, Madrigal Pharmaceuticals has been very busy in continuing to generate sales with its FDA-approved non-cirrhotic MASH drug Rezdiffra. A recent transaction that it had made was to enter into a global license agreement with CSPC Pharmaceutical Group Limited to obtain SYH2086. The truth is that it did not enter into this deal just to be another player in the ever-growing obesity treatment market space with a new oral small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist.

What is the end goal then? The end goal for this company is to generate a superior molecule for the targeting of patients with MASH. That is, to combine SYH2086 together with Rezdiffra to target this group of patients. The rationale for doing such a move is because Madrigal picked something up in the phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH trial, which is that even moderate weight loss achieved increased the anti-fibrotic effect observed in this late-stage study. With a GLP-1 receptor agonist in place like SYH2086 to allow MASH patients to lose weight, it could increase the anti-fibrotic effect impact that is achieved with Rezdiffra. In essence, a once-daily oral pill of this caliber could help Madrigal expand its market potential to go against competing MASH therapies in clinical development or near approval.

The downside is that the company is still in the early stages of clinical testing and with that said a phase 1 trial in the clinic for the combination of SYH2086 and Rezdiffra is not expected to start until the 1st half of 2026. This was not a simple deal, though, and over time Madrigal will have to pony up a lot of cash because of it. Under the terms of the agreement, it was noted that CSPC is to receive approximately $120 million in cash and could also be eligible to earn up to an additional $2 billion in milestone payments. Plus, royalties on net sales for any marketed product from this collaboration agreement. Subject to no issues with regulatory authorities, this license agreement is expected to close in Q4 of 2025. The bottom-line is that both of these clinical drugs could be combined into a once-daily pill that could not only be far more effective than Rezdiffra alone but also safe/tolerable for patients as well.

U.S. Sales Of Rezdiffra Pick Up Along With Move To Pick Up European Territories As Well

The truth is that the company is already seeing massive sales of Rezdiffra for the treatment of adults with non-cirrhotic non-alcoholic steatohepatitis [NASH]. This is based on the fact that in its most recent Q1 of 2025 financial earnings and update report, it noted that it had generated net sales of $137.3 million of this oral once-daily THR-beta agonist. This leads to the 2nd possible expansion opportunity to note where the company could add to its bottom-line earnings. This would be in terms of the ongoing review of Rezdiffra by the EC to determine if it should be approved for adults with non-cirrhotic MASH with moderate to advanced liver fibrosis.

The beauty of this decision is that investors won't have to wait long, because the EC decision is expected in August of 2025. If Madrigal achieves the ability to market this drug in Europe, this would only serve to help boost revenues of Rezdiffra in the coming years. There is a good chance that it will be approved and the reason why I believe this to be the case is because it was notified that the CHMP adopted a positive opinion, recommending that this oral THR-beta agonist be approved to treat this specific MASH patient population. Beyond this scope of this positive CHMP opinion, the drug was shown to achieve both efficacy endpoints of the phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH, achieving both primary endpoints of NASH resolution and fibrosis improvement. The ability to European marketing approval would also generate a "first," so to say. That is, it would be the first therapy to be approved for the European Union [EU] to treat patients with MASH.

Compensated F4 MASH cirrhosis Phase 3 Study Presses On For Confirmation And Label Expansion

The two expansion opportunities of moving Rezdiffra forward in combination with SYH2086 in clinical testing and possible European marketing approval are only the beginning. This brings me to the third and last expansion opportunity that I want to go over for this drug candidate from Madrigal's pipeline. This involves the ongoing development of Rezdiffra in the phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES trial. This study is important for two main reasons. One reason is that it is in place to act as a confirmatory trial, whereby it will convert Accelerated Approval into "full approval" with a positive outcome.

The reason why is because this drug was approved thus far on the basis of a surrogate or intermediate clinical endpoint of Accelerated Approval. In order for the company to continue to sell Rezdiffra for the treatment of adults with non-cirrhotic non-alcoholic steatohepatitis [NASH], the study must meet the primary endpoint over a long period of time. If the study fails to prove clinical benefit, then the FDA could end up pulling this drug off the market. The second item that this phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES trial is supposed to do is expand the label of Rezdiffra. That is, to expand the label to include the targeting of compensated MASH cirrhosis patients [F4c]. One key thing to note though about the potential to expand to this other MASH cirrhosis patient population is that it is going to take some time. That's because the release of topline data from this late-stage study is not expected until early 2027.

It remains to be seen if Madrigal is able to achieve a positive outcome when it comes to its release of final topline data from the ongoing phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES study. However, there were encouraging signs released from an ongoing open-label study, known as Phase 3 MAESTRO-NAFLD-1 trial. This includes two-year open-label trial data showing that treatment with Rezdiffra was able to achieve significant improvements in many clinical biomarkers such as liver stiffness and clinically significant poral hypertension [CSPH]. With respect to CSPH, it was shown that 65% of these MASH patients, who took this drug over a 2-year period, were able to move into lower risk categories. Why is this important? Well, the problem is that CSPH at higher risk categories involves other complications of liver disease such as: Variceal bleeding, abdomen being filled with fluid and hepatic encephalopathy [brain disorder due to liver not being able to remove ammonia from the blood]. This measure of 65% is further broken down into the following:

  • 42% to none/low CSPH
  • 23% to probable CSPH in place.

Another biomarker, which was clinically significant from baseline, was the change in baseline of liver stiffness. Whether or not all of this data translates into a successful outcome of the ongoing phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES study remains to be seen. However, these signs are encouraging and hopefully F4c patients can finally have a drug approved by regulators to help them. Rezdiffra is currently approved to help treat non-cirrhotic MASH F2 [moderate] and F3 [advanced] MASH patients. The move to F4c would allow it to possibly double the amount of revenue it could generate compared to the currently approved indications [Madrigal Pharmaceuticals corporate presentation May 2025, slide #10].

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Madrigal Pharmaceuticals had cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and marketable securities of $848.1 million as of March 31, 2025. The reason for the cash on hand is first and foremost because back in 2024 it entered into a public offering with several big institutions. The net proceeds of this offering, after deducting expenses were said to be $659.9 million. The truth is that this company did not have a lot of cash on hand. Not only does it have to worry about current operating costs, but it is also going to need to use additional capital to expand the marketing of Rezdiffra in other territories.

For instance, if granted European Union marketing approval by the EC, then it is going to need additional funds to market its drug, along with other costs. Plus, in the 10-Q SEC Filing, it notes that it has an outstanding principal amount of its Loan Facility of $115 million with an interest rate of 9.95%. With all of these mounting costs, it needed to raise additional cash and so it did. It did so with the development of a $500 million senior secured credit facility with Blue Owl. The purpose of this was to refinance an existing senior credit facility first and foremost. Plus, the ability to enact all of the several pipeline expansion opportunities I noted directly above. Besides enacting this, it has further financial flexibility. What do I mean by this? Well, that's because it has an at-the-market [ATM] agreement in place it could utilize if it chooses to do so.

This would be a Sales Agreement made with Cowen and Company LLC. in May of 2024 that supersedes a 2021 agreement. It was authorized to sell up to $300 million worth of common stock. The good news with this is that as of March 31, 2025 it still has $300 million it could use under such agreement. The sales are doing fantastic as noted above, whereby Rezdiffra revenues for Q1 of 2025 came in at $137.3 million. Just from Q4 of 2024 to Q1 of 2025, sales of this THR-beta agonist increased by 33%. Plus, the launch of this drug is tracking that of other best-in-class clinical candidates as well [Madrigal Pharmaceuticals Corporate Presentation May of 2025, slide #4]. The company is gearing up to release Q2 of 2025 financials and hold a conference call on Tuesday August 5, 2025. What investors should watch for is whether or not sales of Rezdiffra are continuing to increase.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Madrigal Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be that of the recent exclusive global licensing agreement that the company made with CSPC Pharmaceutical Group Limited to obtain oral small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist SYH2086 for the treatment of patients with MASH. The goal is to start an early-stage study using Rezdiffra combined with SYH2086 for the treatment of patients with MASH in the 1st half of 2026. The risk here is that despite evidence of patients achieving modest weight loss of 5% in the phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH study having seen improved anti-fibrotic activity, there is no assurance that this Rezdiffra combination is going to result in such anti-fibrotic clinical outcome in the expected early stage study. Nor that the clinical results to be produced will be enough to move forward towards phase 2 studies either.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing EC review of Rezdiffra for the treatment of adults with non-cirrhotic MASH with moderate to advanced fibrosis. Even though the CHMP adopted a positive opinion on recommending approval of this THR-beta agonist for the treatment of these MASH patients, there is no guarantee that the EC is going to grant European marketing approval of it. Secondly, there is no assurance that even if the drug is granted marketing approval for this EU territory that it will be able to see an increase in revenues of Rezdiffra because of it.

The third and final risk involves the ongoing advancement of the phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES trial. This risk is broken down into two parts because as I highlighted above, there are two important outcomes from this late-stage trial. The first risk of which is that with a negative outcome from this phase 3 study, the confirmation of benefit of Rezdiffra targeting non-cirrhotic MASH patients with moderate and advanced fibrosis won't be observed. In that scenario, the FDA could decide to pull this drug off the market for the treatment of these MASH patients. A second risk then involves that of a possible label expansion of this drug to be able to market Rezdiffra to compensated MASH cirrhosis [F4c] patients. The risk is that even with positive data obtained from this ongoing phase 3 MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES study, there is no assurance that regulators are going to approve this drug to treat these specific MASH cirrhosis patients.

Conclusion

Madrigal Pharmaceuticals has done well to continue to sell its FDA-approved drug Rezdiffra for the treatment of adults with non-cirrhotic NASH with moderate to advanced fibrosis. This is evidenced above with Q1 of 2025 sales of this drug for the treatment of these patients coming in at $137.3 million. What investors should be on the lookout for next would be the next set of earnings to be released Tuesday August 5, 2025 prior to the financial markets opening.

The premise here is that not only has this company been doing well in terms of revenue being generated with Rezdiffra, but that it has several expansion opportunities. These would be the ability to expand the drug to Europe upon EC approval in August of 2025 [if granted], expand to the compensated MASH cirrhosis pool of patients with the 2027 data release from the MAESTRO-NASH-OUTCOMES study, and then see if the combination of Rezdiffra and licensed SYH2086 results in improved efficacy in MASH. If all of these expansion opportunities play out with a positive outcome, then this company should continue to see sales growth of its flagship drug Rezdiffra for years to come.

data.title: AvalonBay Communities, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by AvalonBay Communities, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Match Group: Attractive Sector Leader Seeking Lost Growth
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Ragmar Rikberg as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Investing in Match Group, Inc. (NASDAQ:MTCH) has been dead money over the past couple of years, with the stock having fallen off most investors’ radars. Markets often associate the company solely with Tinder (and some may have heard of Hinge), dating apps owned by Match, whose active user base has been in decline lately. Investors are often unaware that Match Group is actually consolidating a broad range of the world’s most popular dating apps, and it is a dominant force in the online dating industry, having been a pioneer in the space since 1995.

With approximately 82 million monthly active users, including around 15 million paid subscribers, Match Group holds a strong leading position in the global online dating market, with an estimated share between 30–40%. The closest competitor, Bumble Inc (BMBL), is a distant second with approximately a 10% category share, followed by Grindr Inc. (GRND) with 3-4%. In addition, consumer behavior data generated by such a broad user base is a highly valuable asset in today’s emerging AI-driven economy.

Match Group’s financial profile is strong, with high profitability margins, stable and sustainably growing positive cash flow, and a well-managed debt level. While dating market growth trends have been somewhat muted in the last couple of years following the pandemic-era boom, long-term prospects stay positive, as global growth in online dating users has remained intact.

The company has recently faced a corporate-level shake-up after a well-known value-oriented activist investor, Elliott Management acquired a meaningful stake in the company around one and a half years ago, followed by another value-oriented fund, Starboard Value, in July 2024. They currently hold stakes of approximately 10% and 6.5%, respectively. Actions pushed by these funds have followed — new board appointments have taken place, and both CFO and CEO were replaced by early 2025. These changes may help get the company back on a growth trajectory and restore investor confidence.

Given Match Group’s leading market position, strong financials, and new management, supported by their strong growth-oriented initiatives — including an intensified focus on AI strategies — I believe the current valuation presents a very favorable risk-reward setup. Accordingly, I assign a BUY rating to Match Group, and my projected price target is at least in $50 area within a couple of years timeframe.

From Garage Innovation to Global Dating Empire: Match Group's Rise

What started as a tiny but bold, innovative idea has grown into a $10 billion global powerhouse: today, Tinder is virtually synonymous with online dating, capturing alone an estimated 15–20% of global market share. Available in 190 countries and 40 languages, Tinder operates on an impressive scale. While Tinder is the company's largest piece of pie and brings in around half of their revenues, Match Group’s dominance is far larger than one app

Match Group as a whole holds a portfolio of over 14 leading dating platforms — including Tinder, Hinge, Match.com, OkCupid, Plenty of Fish, Meetic, and OurTime — along with dozens of niche apps and websites, positioning the company as the clear leader in the global online dating industry. While precise market size estimates vary, sources like Grand View Research, Custom Market Insights, and Statista suggest a total addressable online dating market currently of $8–10 billion. Match Group’s annual revenue of ~$3.5 billion implies a commanding 30–40% share of the global market — a powerful level of influence. By comparison, their closest competitor, Bumble, holds around 10% of world's online dating share.

How did they become such dominant leaders?

Here is a brief timeline of key strategic milestones:

1995: Match.com launches, predating Google by three years — the first major online dating site.

1999: Acquired by IAC through its Ticketmaster subsidiary.

2009: IAC formally incorporated Match Group as a holding company for its dating assets.

2011: Acquires OkCupid ($50M) and launches OurTime for singles aged 50+.

2012: Launches Tinder via Hatch Labs (IAC incubator).

2015: Acquires Japanese app Pairs; announces and completes IPO.

2016: Acquisition of Plenty of Fish.

2018: Acquistion of Hinge.

2019: Included to the Fortune 100 list of fastest-growing companies.

2020: IAC spinoff of Match Group; Match added to the Nasdaq-100 index.

2021: Acquisition of Hyperconnect (Korea); joins S&P 500; share price hits all-time high of $182, market cap exceeds $40B.

2025: Spencer Rascoff, Zillow co-founder, became a CEO.

Thirty years of strategic M&A and platform diversification, Match Group has successfully handled demographic shifts, cultural preferences, and global expansion. They have had a powerful influence how people date in the todays digital age.

Recent Developments

Match Group shareholders have had a wild ride in the recent couple of years, with the stock price cratering from its all-time high of approximately $182 in June 2021 to the ~$30 range today.

During COVID-19 times, the stock surged as online dating platforms saw an impressive rise in user engagement. Ultra-low interest rates and booming equity markets drove Match Group to sky-high valuation, culminating in its peak in mid-2021. Since then, the stock has lost nearly 80% of its value, erasing an estimated $40 billion in shareholder wealth.

This dramatic decline raises the question: was it merely a pandemic-era bursting bubble, like what occurred to many online and tech stocks, or have there been any fundamental shifts in the company’s fundamental background also?

Surprisingly, according to company financials, revenue has actually continued its upward trajectory—now over 10% higher than during the stock’s peak. Net income has doubled since that time. Fundamentally, the company is sound in every aspect. The only worrisome issue lies in the rate of growth. Match Group revenue growth has decelerated from over 20% annually in 2021 to low single-digit declines lastly.

Markets are highly sensitive to slowing momentum, and a company transitioning from strong growth to stagnation — or even contraction — often attracts negative sentiment, even if underlying profitability is still solid.

After the initial decline to ~$50 in early 2022, the stock traded sideways within the $30–50 range until reaching its all-time low of $26.39 on April 8, 2025. Analyst raised concerns frequently on earnings calls, centered on stalled growth and even significant revenue decline in the case of Tinder. While management has consistently promised improvements, this has yet to be proven to investors.

In early 2024, activist hedge fund Elliott Management bought ~$1 billion stake in Match Group. Their involvement marked a turning point: following governance changes, the board appointed Spencer Rascoff as CEO on February 4, 2025, replacing Bernard Kim. Rascoff, a Zillow co-founder and former CEO, had joined Match Group’s board in March 2024 alongside Laura Jones, Instacart’s Chief Marketing Officer. Both were brought in to reinvigorate growth and user engagement across the company’s portfolio.

Given Elliott’s long-standing track record of unlocking value in underperforming companies, their involvement, followed by Starboard Value fund around six months later in 2024, presents a reason to reassess Match Group’s potential. This raises an intriguing question: could there be more value beneath the surface than the current share price reflects?

A Closer Look at Financials

Match Group has executed numerous acquisitions over the decades while maintaining disciplined financial management, resulting in a fundamentally sound company. Unlike many fast-growing online platforms that went through aggressive M&A, Match Group has been consistently profitable since its IPO in 2015.

Over the past five years, free cash flow (FCF) has remained notably stable, ranging between $800–900 million annually, with the exception of a dip to ~$500 million in 2022 — a figure that has since rebounded to pre-2022 levels. This level of consistency is uncommon in the often volatile tech sector and highlights Match Group’s strong financial performance.

While Match Group's annual FCF has been in the $800–900 million range, the company’s income statement also includes substantial stock-based compensation expenses, averaging approximately $250 million per year. It’s cash free neutral, but in reality, it goes out of the shareholders' pockets. After making the adjustment, I used a conservative base free cash flow estimate of $600 million for the valuation calculations.

Valuation

To assess the intrinsic share value — and demonstrate how sensitive the projected valuation is to shifts in growth and discount rate assumptions — I applied a Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model using varying discount and growth rates. The key metrics used in the calculation are as follows:

  • Discount rates: 7% and 8%
  • Growth rates: 3%, 4%, 5%, and 6%
  • Base free cash flow: $600 million
  • Net debt: approximately $3 billion
  • Diluted share count: approximately 275 million

The selected discount rates reflect a moderate risk profile in the current interest rate environment, though it’s always some degree of subjective and open to debate. This approach highlights the model’s sensitivity to chosen inputs — even modest shifts in projections can result in significant changes to the implied share price.

Based on this DCF analysis, Match Group’s fundamental calculated share price ranges between $34 and $220, depending on actual growth performance and chosen discount rate. The midpoint between $45 and $103 is a more realistic scenario if they could perform well from a growth perspective. Still, under more favorable interest rate conditions and stronger-than-expected execution, a valuation even exceeding $200 per share isn’t out of sight by the DCF modeling. While this is a rather wide range, all scenarios point to a price just at or above its current ~34 per share at the time of this writing. In other words, the most conservative scenario indicates today's stock price at the lowest end of its fair value.

It’s important to keep in mind that the perpetual growth rate used in the calculations is highly unlikely in real-world scenarios. It is likely to be more volatile and may even contract at times. However, while it is impossible to predict longer-term growth with certainty, GDP tends to average around 2-3% over time. This provides the base for companies to achieve at least a similar growth rate in the longer term if they’re in a naturally growing industry and don’t face some company-specific problems.

From a balance sheet perspective, Match Group maintains approximately $3.4 billion in long-term debt. However, with an EBITDA-to-debt ratio of around 3, its leverage remains at the acceptable area. The company continues to demonstrate disciplined capital allocation by returning value to shareholders — share buybacks have totaled roughly $800 million year-to-date and they also offer an annual dividend yield of approximately 2.3%.

Relative valuation analysis presents some complexity, as selecting appropriate peers is not an easy task. I focused on companies in the online retail subscription-based space with market capitalizations between $3 billion and $30 billion. The selected peers have relatively similar monetization models or user engagement dynamics and are retail consumer oriented:

  • Duolingo – a subscription-based EdTech platform
  • Pinterest Inc. (PINS)– an ad-driven photo-sharing platform
  • Reddit, Inc. (RDDT) – a fast growing social platform where users can interact with others
  • Grindr – LGBTQ+ dating app
  • Maplebear, Inc. (CART), better known as Instacart – a grocery delivery platform

While not perfect comps, they offer insight into how digital platforms with large retail user bases and recurring monetization models are currently valued in the market.
